codings.submission_text:
When we started this unit on intersectionality I have to be honest, I thought it was going to be one of those academic things where they take something everybody already knows and put a complicated word on it. Like obviously people deal with more than one thing at a time. My mom is Black and a woman and works two jobs and is a single parent. She doesn't need a theory to tell her that those things pile up. But then I actually read Crenshaw and I think what changed for me was understanding that it's not just about the piling up. It's about how the specific combination creates something different from any single part.

The example that really hit me was when Crenshaw talks about Black women in the legal system. She shows how discrimination cases kept getting thrown out because the court would say well it's not race discrimination because Black men aren't affected this way, and it's not sex discrimination because white women aren't affected this way. The intersection was literally invisible to the law. That made me think about my mom's experiences at work in a totally new way. She's told me stories about being passed over for promotions and she always frames it as racism, which I'm sure it is. But reading Crenshaw made me wonder if there's something specific about being a Black woman in a management setting that creates a different kind of barrier than what a Black man or a white woman faces in the same company.

I brought this up at dinner and my mom actually got really quiet for a minute. Then she said something like 'I never thought about it that way but yeah, the way they treat me is different from how they treat Darrell' who is her Black male coworker. She said Darrell gets the 'intimidating' treatment where people are scared of him, but she gets the 'invisible' treatment where people just talk over her in meetings like she's not there. Same racism, different shape because of gender. And then she said the really hard part is that when she brings it up, people tell her she's being too sensitive or making it about race when it's really about her personality. Which is a whole other layer of intersection where the complaint itself gets dismissed because of who is making it.

I think this connects to what Professor Davis was saying about how categories are social constructions that have real material effects. Race is constructed, gender is constructed, class is constructed, but the construction doesn't make the effects less real. My mom doesn't experience a socially constructed barrier — she experiences not getting promoted and not being heard in meetings and being told she's too sensitive. The construction is real because the effects are real and the effects reinforce the construction. It's circular in a way that makes it really hard to break.

Something else I keep thinking about is how intersectionality applies within communities too, not just between communities and institutions. In my church there are gender dynamics that are racialized in specific ways and class dynamics that play out differently for the older generation who came from the South versus my generation who grew up here. The intersections are everywhere once you start seeing them, which is both the power and the limitation of the framework. If everything is intersectional, how do you decide where to focus? How do you build a movement when everyone's intersection is different? I don't think Crenshaw fully answers that but I think asking the question is the right starting point.

The last thing I want to say is that this reading made me think about my own position differently. I'm a Black woman but I'm also a high school student with college-educated parents who lives in a relatively safe neighborhood and goes to a school with decent resources. My intersections are real but they're different from my cousin's who dropped out and is working at Walgreens and dealing with a whole different set of compounding pressures. Intersectionality isn't just about the intersections that produce disadvantage — it's about understanding that everyone's position is a specific combination, and that specificity matters for how you understand and respond to the world.

I came into this assignment thinking I already knew what intersectionality was and I'm leaving it realizing I only understood the surface. The framework is deeper than 'people face multiple oppressions.' It's about how the legal system, workplaces, communities, and even our own self-understanding are structured in ways that make certain specific combinations visible or invisible, heard or dismissed, promoted or overlooked. That's what I'm taking from Crenshaw.

codings.submission_text:
Crenshaw's concept of intersectionality fundamentally challenges the way we think about identity and discrimination. Rather than treating race, gender, and class as separate axes of oppression, she argues that these categories overlap and interact to create unique experiences that can't be understood through any single lens. I think this is important because in our current political moment, people tend to reduce everything to one category. You're either fighting racism or fighting sexism, and the people who live at the intersection of both get lost in the shuffle.

The legal cases Crenshaw analyzes are really powerful because they show how this isn't just theoretical. When the courts told Black women that they couldn't bring discrimination claims that weren't purely about race or purely about gender, the law was literally erasing their specific experience. The framework itself created blind spots. I think this has parallels in how schools work too — when they do diversity initiatives they often focus on one axis at a time. There's a Black History Month assembly and a Women's History Month assembly but nobody talks about what it's like to be a Black girl in this school specifically, which is different from being a Black boy here and different from being a white girl here.

I've been thinking a lot about how this plays out in my own community. My neighborhood is mostly Black and Latino and there are definitely tensions between those communities that intersectionality helps explain. It's not just about race because we're all people of color in a predominantly white city. The intersections of immigration status, language, generational history in this country, and which specific stereotypes attach to which group create very different experiences even within the same low-income neighborhood. My Black friends deal with policing in one way, my Mexican friends deal with ICE anxiety in a totally different way, and both groups deal with poverty but the resources available to each are shaped by different institutional histories.

The thing that gets me is how invisible these differences are from the outside. When politicians talk about our neighborhood they just say "underserved community" or "at-risk youth" and those labels flatten everything. They don't see that the Black families on my block have been here for 40 years and the Central American families moved in during the 2000s and the dynamics between those groups are shaped by decades of history that the label "people of color" doesn't begin to capture. My friend Carlos once said something I keep thinking about, that in our neighborhood we're all poor in the same zip code but we're poor in different languages and that changes everything about what resources you can access and what assumptions people make about why you're poor. I think that's an intersectional observation even though Carlos has never read Crenshaw.

Something else I want to mention from the reading is how Crenshaw talks about political intersectionality, not just structural. She argues that the political agendas of different identity groups can work against people at intersections. The feminist movement and the anti-racist movement both claimed to represent Black women but neither actually centered their specific concerns. I see this in my school too. The Black Student Union and the Latino Student Alliance both do important work but a student who is Afro-Latino sometimes feels pulled between them, like they have to choose which part of their identity to organize around. The organizations are structured on single axes and the people at the intersections have to do the work of bridging them.

I want to connect this to the reading more directly but honestly I'm struggling to focus right now. I've been up since 5 AM because I had morning practice, then school, then I went straight to my job at Jamba Juice until 9, and now it's almost midnight and I still have this paper and calc homework. I know I'm supposed to be going deeper with the analysis here but my brain is just not cooperating tonight. I feel like I'm running on nothing and I've been running on nothing for weeks now. Every time I think I'm going to have a weekend to catch up, something else comes up — last weekend it was covering someone else's shift because I can't afford to say no to the hours, and the weekend before that it was a family thing I couldn't miss.

I actually think there's something intersectional about what I'm going through right now, even though it feels weird to apply the framework to myself in the middle of a paper about it. I'm a Black male student athlete who also works to help his family pay rent, and each of those things takes time that the other things need. The school sees me as a student. Practice sees me as an athlete. Jamba Juice sees me as an employee. My mom sees me as the oldest son who's supposed to step up. Nobody sees the whole picture of how impossible it is to be all of those things with the same 24 hours, and when I start dropping any of those balls, I know which one they'll blame — me. Not the situation, not the impossible math of it, but me for not working hard enough or managing my time better. That's an intersection too, even if Crenshaw wasn't writing about teenage boys falling asleep over their laptops.

OK so coming back to the reading. One thing I think Crenshaw gets really right is that intersectionality isn't just about adding up disadvantages. It's about how the specific combination creates experiences that are invisible to frameworks that only look at one thing. My experience right now is invisible to my coach who thinks I'm not giving 100% at practice, invisible to my teachers who think I'm not doing the reading carefully enough, and invisible to my manager who thinks I should be more energetic on shift. Each of them sees one slice and judges me on it.

I guess what I'm saying is that intersectionality isn't just a framework for understanding big systemic issues like Crenshaw's legal cases. It also explains the everyday experience of being pulled in multiple directions by systems that each only see one part of you and expect you to perform as if that's all you are. I know this paper probably isn't my best work and I'm sorry about that. I had more I wanted to say about how the framework connects to the other readings we've done on structural racism and how institutions create and maintain categories. But I need to sleep. I'll try to come to office hours this week to talk about it more if I can find the time.

codings.submission_text:
Crenshaw's intersectionality framework (1989) proposes that the intersection of race and gender creates a qualitatively distinct experience that cannot be understood by examining either axis in isolation. This challenges the additive model of oppression, in which disadvantages are conceptualized as stacking on top of one another, and replaces it with a multiplicative model in which the interaction of categories produces emergent effects. The distinction matters analytically because the additive model implies that removing one form of oppression would proportionally reduce overall disadvantage, while the multiplicative model suggests that the whole is different from the sum of its parts.

The legal cases Crenshaw uses to illustrate this point are particularly effective because they demonstrate how institutional frameworks — in this case, anti-discrimination law — can be structurally incapable of recognizing intersectional experiences. When the court in DeGraffenreid v. General Motors ruled that Black women could not constitute a protected class distinct from Black people or women, it was not simply making a legal error. It was revealing an epistemic limitation of single-axis frameworks: they cannot perceive what exists at the intersection. This has implications beyond law. Any institutional framework that categorizes people along single axes — including educational assessment, healthcare intake, and social services — will systematically fail to recognize the specific circumstances of people who exist at intersections.

From a theoretical perspective, intersectionality can be situated within broader critiques of universalism in feminist and anti-racist scholarship. Collins (1990) makes a related argument about the matrix of domination, and hooks (1984) critiques mainstream feminism for universalizing white women's experiences. Crenshaw's contribution is to make the structural mechanism precise: it is not simply that dominant groups ignore marginalized experiences, but that the categories through which institutions operate are constructed in ways that render intersections invisible. The invisibility is not an oversight — it is a structural feature of how categories work.

I want to push this point further because I think it connects to something we discussed in class about epistemic violence. Spivak (1988) asks whether the subaltern can speak, and her answer is essentially that the structures of knowledge production are organized in ways that prevent certain voices from being heard even when those voices are speaking. Crenshaw's argument is a specific instance of this: the legal structure is organized in ways that prevent certain claims from being cognizable even when those claims are being made. The Black women plaintiffs were speaking — they were literally in court making arguments — but the framework could not hear them. This is epistemic violence in its most precise form: not silence imposed from outside, but a structural incapacity to receive what is being said. I think this has implications for how we do research and how we design institutions. If the categories we use to understand the world are structurally incapable of perceiving certain experiences, then more data or more empathy won't fix the problem. You need different categories, or you need to question the impulse to categorize at all.

I need to step back from the analytical register for a moment because this reading hit me in a way I wasn't expecting and I want to be honest about that. I'm biracial — my dad is Igbo Nigerian and my mom is Chinese American — and I have spent my entire life existing at an intersection that nobody has a category for. In school they ask me to check boxes and I never fit. In my dad's family I'm not Black enough and in my mom's family I'm not Chinese enough and in American racial discourse I'm either an honorary Black person or an honorary Asian person depending on which conversation is happening, but never just myself.

What Crenshaw's framework gave me — and this is why I'm writing this at 1 AM instead of sleeping because I can't stop thinking about it — is a way to understand that the problem isn't me. The problem is the categories. When I feel like I don't fit, that's not a personal failing, it's the categories doing exactly what Crenshaw says they do: rendering intersections invisible. I've been carrying this feeling of not-fitting-anywhere since I was a kid and I always thought it meant something was wrong with me, like I needed to pick a side or try harder to belong somewhere. But what if the feeling of not fitting is actually accurate information about how identity categories work? What if I'm not failing to be something — what if the categories are failing to capture what I am?

My parents met at a church in Oakland and their marriage was already an intersection that both families struggled with. My dad's family worried he was losing his culture and my mom's family worried about — I don't even know exactly what. Something about respectability and what the community would think. And then I came along, this kid who looked like neither family expected and who grew up code-switching between three cultural contexts without fully belonging to any of them. I used to think that was just my weird family situation but now I think it's actually a textbook case of what happens when people live at intersections that institutions and communities haven't built categories for. The institution doesn't malfunction — it functions exactly as designed, and the design excludes people like me.

So here is where I try to connect the personal back to the theoretical, which is what I think this assignment is actually asking for. Crenshaw's framework was developed specifically around Black women's experiences in the American legal system, and there's a legitimate question about whether it travels to every possible intersection. My experience as a biracial person is not the same as a Black woman's experience in employment law, and I don't want to appropriate the framework by pretending it maps directly. But I think the structural insight does transfer: whenever institutions rely on single-axis categories, the people who exist at intersections will be systematically unseen. That is true whether the intersection is race and gender (Crenshaw's original case), race and disability, immigration status and language, or multiracial identity and belonging.

What I want to push back on slightly is the way intersectionality is sometimes taught as a checklist — how many boxes can you check, how many oppressions do you carry, as if identity is an additive score. That flattens the framework into exactly the additive model Crenshaw was arguing against. The point is not how many categories you occupy. The point is that the interaction between categories creates experiences that the categories themselves cannot capture. That is what I've been living and what the reading finally gave me language for. I don't know if this paper is what was expected but it's what I needed to write tonight.

codings.submission_text: Crenshaw's intersectionality is one of those concepts that I think gets oversimplified a lot in the way it circulates in popular discourse and I want to use this paper to actually dig into what she's saying rather than just repeat the basic version. The basic version goes something like: people have multiple identities and those identities interact to shape their experience. Fine, that's true, but that's also kind of obvious and if that were all Crenshaw was saying it wouldn't be a major intellectual contribution. What makes the framework actually powerful is the structural argument underneath the identity talk, which is that institutional categories are designed around single-axis assumptions and those assumptions create systematic blind spots that harm the people who live at intersections. The operative word is systematic — it's not about individual prejudice or even about the accumulation of disadvantages, it's about how the architecture of institutions renders certain experiences structurally invisible. The legal cases are the proof. In DeGraffenreid the court didn't say Black women don't face discrimination. It said the legal framework has no mechanism for recognizing discrimination that is simultaneously racial and gendered as a distinct form. The framework itself produces the invisibility. That's a much deeper claim than "people face multiple oppressions" and I think it gets lost when intersectionality becomes a buzzword. I want to extend this to education because I think schools do the same thing the courts did. Schools have programs for students of color and programs for low-income students and programs for English learners and programs for students with disabilities, but these programs operate on single-axis assumptions. A student who is a low-income English learner of color with a learning disability doesn't get four supports — they get whichever program captures them first and then the other programs say that student is already being served. The intersection of all four conditions creates a specific experience that none of the individual programs are designed to address. I know this because it basically describes several of my cousins who went through the system and got bounced between programs without anyone ever looking at the whole picture. This is not a failing of the individual programs or the individual counselors. It's a structural feature of how categorical programs work, which is exactly Crenshaw's point. Now here's where I think Crenshaw's framework needs to be pushed further. She developed it primarily around the intersection of race and gender for Black women in America, which is an incredibly important and specific argument. But once you understand the structural mechanism — that single-axis categories produce systematic blind spots — you can see it operating everywhere, including in places that complicate the original framework's political commitments. For example, immigration status intersects with race in ways that don't map cleanly onto the Black-white binary that a lot of American racial analysis is built on. My family is Mexican American and in my neighborhood the intersections of legal status, language, generation, skin color, and class create a complex web of positions that the simple version of intersectionality can't capture. My grandmother who is undocumented and dark-skinned has a completely different relationship to American institutions than my uncle who is a citizen, light-skinned, and college-educated, even though they're both "Mexican." The category "Mexican" is doing so much work that it obscures more than it reveals, which is Crenshaw's point applied recursively to the very categories that intersectionality names. I think this is where the framework becomes really powerful — when you use it not just to analyze the intersection of pre-existing categories but to question whether the categories themselves are adequate to the complexity of lived experience. That's what I took from this reading and I think it's the part that matters most.

codings.submission_text:
When I read the Crenshaw article about intersectionality it was the first time I see an American academic writing about something that my family already know from our life. In Iraq, before we come here, my mother was a woman and she was Sunni in a Shia area and she was also a teacher which means she was educated but not rich and all of these things together they make her experience very specific. It is not just that she is a woman in Iraq, because a rich Shia woman in Baghdad has a completely different life. It is the combination of being Sunni and being a woman and being educated but with no money and being from Mosul specifically that created her situation. When the conflict got worse it was all of these things together that made it dangerous for us, not any one thing by itself. I think Crenshaw would say my mother was at an intersection that the systems could not see because they only look at one thing at a time. The UN people when we apply for refugee status they ask about religion and they ask about the war but they don't ask about what it means to be a Sunni woman teacher from Mosul with children, which is a very specific kind of danger. My mother she had colleagues who were Sunni men and they were also in danger but a different kind of danger. She had colleagues who were Shia women and they were more safe. The intersection is what Crenshaw talks about and I recognize it immediately even though she is writing about Black women in America and not about Iraqi refugees. I think the framework is bigger than America even if it was made in America. When I come to this country I also experience a new intersection that did not exist before. In Iraq I was not Muslim in the way Americans think of Muslim. I was Sunni, I was from a specific family, I was from a specific city. Here I am just Muslim and that category it flatten everything about who I am into one word that Americans associate with terrorism and oppression of women. So now my intersection is: refugee, Muslim, woman, ESL, Arab, and each of these words carries a weight in America that it did not carry in Iraq. The word refugee here means something about being poor and desperate. The word Muslim means something about being dangerous or backwards. The word Arab means something about not being modern. None of these meanings are true about me or my family but they are real in the sense that they affect how people treat me, which is what Crenshaw means I think when she says that categories have material effects even when they are social constructions. My mother in Iraq was not oppressed by the word Sunni — she was oppressed by the specific political and military situation that the word Sunni indexed. Here in America she is not oppressed by the word Muslim exactly — she is oppressed by the specific set of assumptions and policies and surveillance that the word Muslim triggers in the American context. The word changes countries and changes meaning but the mechanism is the same: you are reduced to a category and the category determines how institutions treat you and the category cannot see the full complexity of who you are.

The part of Crenshaw that I am less sure about is whether the framework can account for what happens when you move between systems. My mother's intersection in Iraq was dangerous but at least it was legible to the people around her — everyone in Mosul understood what it meant to be who she was. Here the intersection is illegible. Americans do not have the context to understand what being a Sunni woman from Mosul means, so they substitute their own categories: Muslim, refugee, immigrant, ESL. These substituted categories create a new intersection that has nothing to do with the original one but is equally powerful in determining her access to jobs, housing, respect, and safety. I want to call this something like intersectional translation — the process by which a person's identity is translated from one categorical system to another when they cross borders, and the translation is always a reduction because the receiving system does not have the categories to capture the original complexity. I don't know if this is already a concept in the literature because I have not read everything yet but it is what I observe in my own family's experience. My grandmother in Iraq was a respected elder with social authority. Here she is an old woman who doesn't speak English and needs help filling out forms. Same person, different categorical system, completely different social position. Intersectionality explains each position but I think something else is needed to explain the translation between them. Maybe this is what the professor means by the social construction of categories — that the categories are not just descriptions of reality but are tools that each society builds for its own purposes, and when you move between societies you experience the arbitrariness of the tools directly. That is what my family's experience has taught me and I think Crenshaw's framework is a good starting point for understanding it even if it does not go all the way to where my experience takes me.

She's not just abstractly understanding intersectionality; she's using it to analyze power dynamics within families facing the pressures of undocumented status. The way she connects Crenshaw's legal arguments to the specific challenges faced by Mexican and Central American families is really insightful. She's identifying a gap – a systemic failure – where institutions designed to address single issues (domestic violence, immigration, legal aid) are inadequate when confronted with the complexities of intersectional experiences.

What's striking is her ability to articulate the ways in which these systems, despite the best efforts of the people working within them, are fundamentally built for a "single-axis person." She's not criticizing individuals; she's pointing to the structural limitations of these systems. Her description of how undocumented status can intensify power dynamics within families, creating isolation and limiting options, is powerfully observed. The fact that she chose to write about this, rather than attempting a more "standard analytical response," speaks to a deep commitment to centering the experiences of her community. It's a courageous choice, and it reveals a sophisticated understanding of how academic frameworks can sometimes fail to capture the nuances of lived realities.

Marisol is grappling with the profound difference between understanding intersectionality as a theoretical framework and living it. She's powerfully articulating that Crenshaw's legal analysis, while insightful, doesn't fully capture the existential reality for those navigating multiple, compounding systems of oppression. The core of her argument is a distinction between analytical knowledge (Crenshaw’s) and lived knowledge (her mother’s), recognizing that both are valuable but produce different kinds of understanding.

What’s striking is the raw honesty and vulnerability in her writing, particularly her decision to share a deeply personal story about her family. She explicitly acknowledges the discomfort and potential risk in doing so, stating, "I don't think I'm supposed to be writing about this in a school paper and I'll probably delete this paragraph before I submit." This reveals a keen awareness of boundaries and a complex relationship with academic writing – a desire to connect theory to reality colliding with a sense of impropriety or fear.

Marisol is demonstrating a sophisticated understanding of Crenshaw's argument about the limitations of single-axis frameworks, extending it to critique the fragmented nature of social services. She's not simply restating Crenshaw; she's using Crenshaw's work to illuminate a very specific, painful reality within her own family, and then reflecting on the different kinds of knowledge that arise from that experience. The teacher should notice her ability to connect abstract theory to concrete experience, and her insightful observation about the different forms of knowledge produced by those inside and outside of an experience.

Marisol is grappling with the limitations of academic frameworks when applied to lived experience, particularly when that experience is shaped by multiple intersecting oppressions. She's acutely aware of the gap between theoretical understanding and practical survival, and she's pushing back against the idea that academic analysis should be prioritized over direct engagement with the complexities of those experiences. Her insight about her mother living "inside the gap that the framework describes" is a powerful observation, and her comparison of the feminist movement and domestic violence advocacy reveals a critical understanding of how mainstream institutions often fail those on the margins.

codings.submission_text:
Intersectionality as a framework asks us to consider how overlapping social identities relate to systems of oppression and privilege. Crenshaw developed this concept by examining how Black women's experiences in the workplace and in the legal system couldn't be adequately addressed by either race-based or gender-based frameworks alone. What I found compelling about the reading is not just the argument itself but the method — she uses specific legal cases to show how the gaps in the framework aren't accidental. They're built into how the categories work.

I think about this in terms of my own community. I grew up in a neighborhood where almost everyone is Mexican or Central American, and within that community there are so many intersections that outsiders don't see. There are differences between families who have been here for three generations and families who just arrived. There are differences between people with papers and people without. There are differences between people from different regions of Mexico who carry different racial positions — my abuelo always talked about how being from Oaxaca meant being seen as indigenous in Mexico City, which carried a specific kind of stigma that people from Guadalajara didn't experience. All of these differences intersect with gender and class and language and education and age in ways that create very specific positions.

One thing the reading made me think about is how the concept of machismo intersects with immigration status to create very specific power dynamics within families. This isn't something I've seen discussed much in our class materials, probably because it's an internal community dynamic rather than an external institutional one. But Crenshaw's framework applies here too — the intersection creates something qualitatively different from either machismo alone or immigration stress alone. When a family is under the pressure of undocumented status, the power dynamics inside the household can intensify because the family is isolated from outside support systems. You can't call the police if you don't trust the police. You can't leave if leaving means losing your housing and your kids' stability.

I think Crenshaw would recognize what I'm describing here even though her original cases were about employment discrimination. The mechanism is the same: when you exist at an intersection that institutional support systems weren't designed for, the gap becomes a trap. Domestic violence services assume you can engage with law enforcement. Immigration services don't deal with family safety. Legal aid is overloaded and underfunded and the waitlist alone takes months. Each system was designed for a single-axis person with a single-axis problem, and my community is full of people whose problems are intersectional in ways that no single system can address. This isn't a criticism of the people who work in those systems — many of them are doing incredible work with limited resources. It's a criticism of the categorical architecture, the same way Crenshaw was critiquing the architecture of anti-discrimination law, not the individual judges.

I'm writing about this because it's real for me, not theoretical. My mom has been dealing with my stepdad for years and it got worse after my uncle got deported because that scared everyone in the family and made them feel like we had to keep everything inside, keep quiet, don't draw attention. My stepdad knows my mom won't go to anyone for help because she's afraid and he uses that. The intersection Crenshaw writes about — where the specific combination of identities creates a trap that neither identity alone explains — that's exactly what I see. It's not just that my mom is a woman dealing with a controlling partner. It's that she's an undocumented woman in a community where asking for help means risking everything, partnered with someone who understands exactly how trapped she is. I don't think I'm supposed to be writing about this in a school paper and I'll probably delete this paragraph before I submit but right now I need to get it out because the reading made it impossible to pretend this is just theory.

What Crenshaw shows in the legal cases is that institutions designed to help people with one problem at a time can't help people whose problems are intersectional. The domestic violence shelter might not have bilingual staff. The immigration lawyer doesn't do family law. The school counselor doesn't know about any of this because we don't talk about it. Each system sees one piece and none of them see how the pieces combine to create a situation where leaving is more dangerous than staying, at least that's how my mom sees it.

I want to go back to the analytical point because I think there's something important here about how intersectionality functions differently for people inside the experience versus people studying it from outside. For Crenshaw, as a legal scholar, the insight is analytical — she can show how legal frameworks fail intersectional subjects. For my mom, the insight is existential — she lives inside the gap that the framework describes. I don't think either perspective is more valid but they produce very different kinds of knowledge. The academic version produces theories and policies. The lived version produces survival strategies that never make it into the literature.

The reading also talks about how the feminist movement has historically centered white women's experiences, and I see a parallel in how domestic violence advocacy often centers middle-class white women's experiences. The advice is always: call the police, go to a shelter, get a restraining order. Those steps assume a relationship to institutions that my mom doesn't have. They assume that the police are safe, that a shelter won't ask about your status, that a restraining order can be enforced when your abuser knows you won't go to court. The intersection of immigration status and domestic violence creates a specific trap that mainstream advocacy frameworks weren't built to address. Some organizations understand this — I know there are groups that specifically serve undocumented DV survivors — but the default framework still assumes a single-axis subject who can access institutions without risk.

I don't know how to end this paper. I started it thinking I would write a standard analytical response to the reading and instead I wrote about my family, which is probably not what was asked for. But Crenshaw says that intersectional experiences are made invisible by single-axis frameworks, and I think staying in the analytical register would have been its own form of single-axis thinking — the axis being "academic." Sometimes the most honest engagement with a text about visibility is to let yourself be visible. I hope that's OK.

codings.submission_text:
The power of Crenshaw's intersectionality framework, as I understand it, is not just that it names the overlap of identities but that it reveals a specific structural problem: institutions built on single-axis categories systematically fail the people who exist at intersections. I want to examine this structural argument closely because I think it has implications beyond what Crenshaw originally intended.

First, the legal argument. Crenshaw shows that anti-discrimination law operates by comparing plaintiffs to a reference group. Race discrimination is measured against the treatment of white people. Sex discrimination is measured against the treatment of men. But for a Black woman, both comparisons are insufficient because her treatment may be distinct from both Black men and white women. The legal framework literally cannot see her experience because it lacks the conceptual categories to do so. This is not a failure of individual judges — it's a failure of the categorical architecture itself.

I find this argument compelling because it applies well beyond the courtroom. Consider medical research. Clinical trials have historically enrolled predominantly white male subjects and then generalized the results. When researchers began disaggregating data by race, they found differences. When they disaggregated by sex, they found differences. But the intersectional question — are there specific health outcomes for Black women that differ from both Black men and white women — is often still unasked because the research design doesn't create space for it. The same structural blindness Crenshaw identifies in law operates in medicine, in education, in technology design, in urban planning.

This brings me to a question about the limits of the framework. If intersectionality identifies the problem — that single-axis categories create blind spots — what does the solution look like institutionally? You can't create a separate legal category for every possible intersection because the number of intersections is combinatorially explosive. Black women, Black disabled women, Black disabled immigrant women, Black disabled immigrant queer women — at some point the categories become so specific that they defeat the purpose of having categories at all. I think this is where Crenshaw's framework is strongest as a diagnostic tool and weakest as a prescriptive one. It tells you what's wrong with how institutions categorize people but doesn't quite tell you how to build institutions that don't need those categories.

One possible direction is what some scholars call an anti-categorical approach: rather than creating more intersections within the existing system, question whether categorical thinking itself is the right framework for addressing inequality. This appeals to me philosophically but I recognize it may not be practical. Institutions need categories to function. You can't distribute resources without some way of identifying who needs them. The question is whether you can build categories that are responsive to intersectionality rather than blind to it.

I think the reading pairs well with what we discussed about structural racism last week. Structural racism is the idea that racial inequality is maintained through institutional systems rather than individual prejudice. Intersectionality adds precision to this claim: it's not just that institutions are structurally racist, it's that the structure of institutional categories themselves creates specific patterns of invisibility that disproportionately harm people at intersections. The structure isn't just racist — it's categorically reductive in a way that produces racism as one of its effects.

This framework changes how I think about activism too. If the problem is categorical, then activism that works within existing categories — demanding better treatment for women, or for people of color, or for LGBTQ people — is necessary but insufficient. You also need activism that challenges the categories themselves, that demands institutions account for the people who fall between the boxes. That's harder to organize around because it doesn't have a single constituency. Intersectional activism is almost a contradiction in terms — how do you build a movement around the insight that movements based on single categories will inevitably leave people out?

I don't have an answer to that but I think Crenshaw's framework is the right starting point. You have to name the problem before you can solve it, and the problem is that our institutions were built for single-axis people who don't exist. Everyone exists at an intersection. The framework that finally accounts for that is the framework that might actually produce just institutions. Whether we can get there from here is the open question that I hope we keep exploring in this class.

feedback.draft_text: Aaliyah, it was really insightful to read your reflections on Crenshaw's work, especially when you said, 'I came into this assignment thinking I already knew what intersectionality was and I'm leaving it realizing I only understood the surface.' That kind of honest self-assessment is a powerful starting point for deeper learning. You did a great job connecting Crenshaw's framework to your family's experiences, showing how race and gender combine to create unique challenges. I'm wondering, as you consider how the effects reinforce the construction, could you explore how those constructions are actively maintained and reproduced within your community?

David, I was really struck by your observation that Crenshaw's intersectionality is often oversimplified in popular discourse – that's a really insightful point about how complex theories can get flattened. You've done a great job applying Crenshaw's ideas to the education system, showing a clear understanding of how institutions can create structural invisibility. Thinking about how categories like 'Mexican' can obscure more than they reveal is a powerful move. I'm wondering, how might Crenshaw's framework help us understand the ways that different systems (like education, healthcare, or the legal system) interact to shape experiences?

Fatima, I was really struck by your concept of 'intersectional translation' – it's a powerful way to articulate the loss of nuance that happens when navigating new systems. You've done a wonderful job connecting Crenshaw's framework to your family's experiences, particularly the contrast between your grandmother's authority in Iraq and her experience in America. As you consider how societies 'build categories for their own purposes,' could you explore how those purposes might reinforce existing power structures?

Jaylen, I was really struck by your point about how different social justice movements can inadvertently exclude those at the intersection of those identities – that's a really insightful observation. It takes courage to be so honest about the challenges you're facing, and I appreciate you sharing your experiences navigating so many expectations. I'm wondering, as you were reflecting on Crenshaw's work, did you notice any parallels between the legal cases she discussed and the ways institutions might unintentionally create barriers for people today?

Marisol, I was really struck by your observation that staying in a purely analytical register could be its own form of single-axis thinking – that's a really insightful point about the limitations of academic frameworks! It takes a lot of courage to bring such personal experiences into the classroom, and I appreciate you sharing your reflections on your family's experiences. I'm wondering, as you thoughtfully considered the tension between academic expectations and the urgency of your family's situation, what other approaches might help bridge the gap between theory and lived experience?

Natasha, I was really struck by your observation that 'the interaction between categories creates experiences that the categories themselves cannot capture' – that's a really insightful way to articulate the limitations of trying to neatly define experiences. It takes a lot of courage and vulnerability to connect such complex theoretical concepts to your own lived experience, and I appreciate you sharing your reflections on navigating those spaces. Thinking about your point about wanting to avoid 'appropriating the framework,' I'm wondering if you could elaborate on how Crenshaw's work might help us understand the nuances of those boundaries you're drawing?

Noah, I was really struck by your observation that 'the categories become so specific that they defeat the purpose of having categories at all' – that's a really insightful point about the limitations of categorical systems. You've done a great job connecting Crenshaw's legal argument to broader structural issues like structural racism. I'm wondering, when you mention that institutions need categories to function, could you explore some alternative ways of thinking about resource distribution that might move beyond rigid categories?

observation_synthesis:
Okay, here's a class summary based on my reading of the student submissions. I've aimed for a 5-minute read, focusing on actionable insights for you.

## Class Temperature

This class is humming with a really exciting level of intellectual curiosity and a genuine desire to grapple with complex ideas. There's a palpable sense of students moving beyond simply understanding *what* intersectionality is to actively questioning *how* it works, *where* it falls short, and *what* it can be used for. The energy feels driven by a combination of intellectual challenge and a desire to connect theory to lived experience.

## What Students Are Reaching For

Here are the main intellectual threads I'm seeing:

*   **Marisol Delgado-Reyes:** Marisol’s decision to center her family’s experiences in her writing, even if it meant deviating from a "standard analytical response," is a powerful testament to her authenticity and courage. She articulates how mainstream frameworks can fail to address the specific needs of marginalized communities, and her honesty about the challenges of translating theory into action is incredibly valuable. *Why it matters:* This demonstrates a commitment to centering marginalized voices and challenging conventional academic expectations. *Mirror back:* Validate her decision to prioritize visibility and encourage this kind of self-reflection.
*   **Fatima Al-Rashidi:** Fatima’s connection to her family’s experiences in Iraq provides a rich context for understanding the complexities of navigating multiple forms of oppression. Her grappling with the question of what *else* is needed to explain the translation of social positions across different cultural contexts is a very sophisticated question. *Why it matters:* This shows a willingness to push beyond the boundaries of Crenshaw’s framework and engage with broader questions about social construction and power. *Mirror back:* Encourage her expansive thinking and acknowledge the value of her questions.
*   **Natasha Okafor-Chen:** Natasha’s critique of the 'checklist' approach to intersectionality, combined with her connection to Spivak’s concept of epistemic violence, demonstrates a nuanced understanding of Crenshaw’s core argument and a capacity for synthesizing theoretical concepts. *Why it matters:* This shows a critical awareness of how institutions, even those aiming for inclusivity, can inadvertently exclude those who exist at multiple intersections. *Mirror back:* Validate her shift to personal reflection and encourage this kind of self-reflection.

## Moments for the Classroom

Several students are wrestling with the tension between the theoretical framework of intersectionality and the practical realities of social change. Some are questioning how to move beyond analysis to action, while others are grappling with the challenges of organizing around intersectional issues. This tension could be explored in a class discussion by asking students to brainstorm concrete examples of how Crenshaw’s framework can be applied to real-world problems.

## Structural Power Moves to Address

*   **Colorblind Erasure:** Several students, including David Orozco and Natasha Okafor-Chen, demonstrated a subtle tendency towards colorblind erasure when discussing their family experiences. For example, David's framing of his family's experience as a challenge to the "simple version of intersectionality" risks minimizing the specific ways that anti-Blackness and other forms of racialization operate within Latinx communities. *Pedagogical approach:* In a class discussion, you could gently challenge the assumption that a more "complex" understanding of intersectionality necessarily involves erasing the specificity of racial experiences. You could ask students to consider how different forms of oppression interact and reinforce one another.
*   **Abstract Liberalism:** Aaliyah's framing of the "construction" of social categories as "real because the effects are real" and Fatima's discussion of institutions building categories "for their own purposes" both reflect a subtle tendency towards abstract liberalism. *Pedagogical approach:* You could ask students to consider the role of power in shaping and maintaining social categories. You could ask them to consider who benefits from these categories and how they are used to justify inequality.



## Looking Ahead to Next Week

Given the strong engagement with Crenshaw’s framework, students are well-positioned to engage with Omi & Winant’s concept of racial formation. The students who are grappling with the limitations of categories will be particularly interested in Omi & Winant’s argument that race is a socially constructed system that is constantly being negotiated and contested. Fatima Al-Rashidi’s questions about the translation of social positions across different societies will be particularly relevant to Omi & Winant’s discussion of how racial categories are shaped by historical and political contexts. Encourage students to consider how Crenshaw’s framework and Omi & Winant’s framework can be used together to analyze the complexities of race and inequality.
